title: Quantization & Mixed Precision
summary: Reduce numerical precision: float32 → float16/bfloat16 → int8 → int4. Less memory, faster compute, minimal quality loss. Mixed precision: compute in float16, accumulate in float32. Post-training quantization (PTQ) vs quantization-aware training (QAT).
key_insights: float32: 4 bytes per param. float16: 2 bytes. int8: 1 byte. int4: 0.5 bytes. 7B model: 28GB → 14GB → 7GB → 3.5GB.
Mixed precision training: forward/backward in float16 (fast), weight updates in float32 (precise). Loss scaling prevents gradient underflow.
bfloat16 (Google): same exponent range as float32 (no overflow), reduced mantissa. Better for training than float16.
Post-training quantization (PTQ): train in float32, quantize afterward. Simple but some quality loss.
Quantization-aware training (QAT): simulate quantization during training. Model learns to be robust. Better quality, more effort.
GPTQ / AWQ / GGML: popular PTQ methods for LLMs. 4-bit quantization with minimal quality loss for inference.

mathematical_core.linear_quantization: $$q = \text{round}\left(\frac{x - z}{s}\right), \quad \hat{x} = s \cdot q + z$$ where $s$ = scale, $z$ = zero point
mathematical_core.mixed_precision: Forward: $y = f_{\text{fp16}}(x)$. Loss: $L_{\text{fp32}} = \text{scale} \cdot L_{\text{fp16}}$. Gradients: fp16. Update: $\theta_{\text{fp32}} -= \eta \cdot g_{\text{fp32}}$.

derivations.title: Quantization error analysis

steps.name: Round-trip error
steps.math: $|x - \hat{x}| \leq s/2$ where $s$ = scale factor. Maximum error = half a quantization step.

steps.name: Per-layer impact
steps.math: Error compounds through layers: $\epsilon_{\text{output}} \approx \sum_l \|J_l\| \cdot \epsilon_l$. Sensitive layers (attention, first/last) need higher precision.

steps.name: Outlier problem
steps.math: If one weight is 100× larger than others: $s$ is large → all other weights lose precision. Solution: per-channel quantization or outlier handling (LLM.int8()).

derivations.consequence: Quantization works because: (1) most weights cluster near zero, (2) networks are over-parameterized (can tolerate noise), (3) per-channel scales handle outliers.

related: PRODUCTION.DISTILLATION — can combine: distill then quantize

notes: Directly relevant to Anoop's local-first AI tool: Ollama models are typically GGUF-quantized (4-bit or 5-bit). Understanding quantization helps judge quality tradeoffs for local vs cloud routing.
